questions.stem: A 25-year-old medical student presents with intense itching, particularly at night, for 3 weeks. His partner has similar symptoms. On examination, there are tiny burrows in the web spaces of his fingers and erythematous papules on his wrists and elbows. What is the single most likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation: The presence of burrows, nocturnal itch, and contact history are highly characteristic of scabies. Atopic eczema typically involves flexures and lacks burrows.

questions.stem: A 78-year-old man from a nursing home presents with thick, crusted plaques on his hands, feet, and scalp. He reports minimal itch. Several residents and staff have developed an itchy rash. Skin scrapings show numerous mites. What is the single most likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation: Crusted scabies presents with hyperkeratotic plaques, minimal pruritus, and a high mite burden, often in elderly or immunocompromised patients. Psoriasis would have silvery scales and no contact history.

questions.stem: A 2-year-old child presents with intensely itchy papules and vesicles on the trunk, limbs, palms, and soles. The mother reports the child is irritable at night. No burrows are seen. There is no family history of atopy. What is the single most likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation: In infants, scabies often presents with widespread papulovesicular lesions involving palms and soles, and burrows may be absent. Atopic dermatitis usually spares palms and soles.

questions.stem: A 40-year-old man completed treatment for scabies with permethrin 5% cream two weeks ago. He now complains of persistent itching, especially at night. He has had no new contact with infected individuals. Examination reveals dry skin and some excoriations but no burrows or new papules. What is the single most likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation: Post-scabetic itch can persist for weeks due to ongoing hypersensitivity. The absence of burrows and new lesions makes active infestation less likely.

questions.stem: A 28-year-old pregnant woman at 20 weeks gestation complains of severe itching, worse at night, with a rash on her abdomen, wrists, and between fingers. She is otherwise well. What is the single most likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation: The distribution (wrists, interdigital) and nocturnal itch point to scabies. Polymorphic eruption of pregnancy typically starts in striae later in pregnancy, and cholestasis lacks a rash.

questions.stem: A 28-year-old man presents with intense itching, worse at night, and a rash between his fingers and on his wrists. On examination, you see several linear, greyish burrows. What is the most appropriate initial diagnostic test to confirm scabies?
questions.explanation: Dermatoscopy is a non-invasive, sensitive first-line test that can visualize the characteristic 'delta-wing sign' of scabies mites. Skin scraping is invasive and less sensitive in early cases, while other options are not first-line for diagnosis.

questions.stem: A 45-year-old woman has had widespread pruritus and an eczematous rash for 6 weeks without obvious burrows. Initial dermatoscopy was equivocal. Which dermoscopic feature would be most specific for confirming scabies?
questions.explanation: The delta-wing sign (a small brown triangle at the end of a burrow) represents the scabies mite's head and is pathognomonic on dermoscopy. Other findings are non-specific or seen in other conditions.

questions.stem: A 65-year-old man with HIV (CD4 80) presents with thick, crusted plaques on his hands, feet, and scalp. A skin scraping is performed. Which microscopic finding would confirm the diagnosis of crusted scabies?
questions.explanation: Crusted scabies is characterized by a very high mite burden; a scraping typically shows hundreds to thousands of mites and eggs. Seeing a single mite or few eggs is more consistent with classic scabies.

questions.stem: A 60-year-old care home resident presents with a widespread, excoriated rash with impetiginized lesions. Secondary infection obscures classic burrows. Which test would be most helpful to confirm underlying scabies?
options: Dermatoscopy of interdigital spaces and wrists
Skin biopsy of an infected lesion
Scabies PCR of a crusted area
questions.explanation: Dermatoscopy can detect residual burrows or mites in unaffected areas such as interdigital spaces even when secondary infection is present. Skin biopsy is less sensitive, and other tests do not directly identify scabies.

questions.stem: An outbreak of itching is reported in a nursing home. Several residents have pruritus and rashes. According to UK guidelines (NICE), what is the essential diagnostic criterion to confirm a scabies outbreak?
options: Positive skin scraping from a symptomatic resident
Dermatoscopy shows burrows in at least two residents
questions.explanation: Confirmation of scabies requires identification of mites, eggs, or faeces on microscopy of a skin scraping. A single confirmed case defines an outbreak; dermatoscopy is supportive but not diagnostic.

questions.stem: A 28-year-old man presents with intense itching, especially at night, and burrows in the web spaces of his fingers. He has no significant past medical history and is not taking any medications. The diagnosis of scabies is confirmed. What is the most appropriate first-line treatment?
options: Topical crotamiton 10% cream, applied on two consecutive nights
Permethrin 5% cream, two applications one week apart
Malathion 0.5% aqueous lotion, two applications one week apart
Benzyl benzoate 25% lotion, applied for 24 hours
Oral ivermectin 200 mcg/kg, two doses one week apart
questions.explanation: Permethrin 5% cream is the first-line treatment for typical scabies in the UK (NICE/BNF). Malathion is an alternative if permethrin is not tolerated or contraindicated. Ivermectin is unlicensed and reserved for crusted scabies or institutional outbreaks.

questions.stem: A 32-year-old pregnant woman (28 weeks gestation) presents with generalized itching and burrows typical of scabies. She is otherwise well. Which of the following treatments is contraindicated in pregnancy?
questions.explanation: Oral ivermectin is contraindicated in pregnancy and breastfeeding due to potential fetal harm. Permethrin and malathion are considered safe in pregnancy, with permethrin being first-line. Crotamiton and sulfur are alternative topical options but not first-line.

questions.stem: A 55-year-old immunocompromised man (on long-term oral corticosteroids for sarcoidosis) presents with widespread thick hyperkeratotic plaques on his hands, feet, and scalp, with burrows and numerous mites. A diagnosis of crusted (Norwegian) scabies is made. What is the most appropriate treatment?
options: Malathion 0.5% aqueous lotion twice weekly for two weeks
Oral ivermectin 200 mcg/kg as a single dose
Permethrin 5% cream plus oral ivermectin (multiple doses)
Permethrin 5% cream applied daily for one week
questions.explanation: Crusted scabies requires aggressive treatment with multiple applications of topical permethrin combined with oral ivermectin (unlicensed but recommended by NICE). Single therapy with either agent alone is insufficient due to high mite burden.

questions.stem: A 6-week-old infant presents with a papular rash on the palms and soles, with burrows. Scabies is confirmed. Which of the following treatments is contraindicated in this age group?
questions.explanation: Malathion is contraindicated in infants under 6 months of age. Permethrin can be used with caution under specialist advice, but sulfur, benzyl benzoate, and crotamiton are not first-line and may have safety concerns; malathion is specifically contraindicated.

questions.stem: A 24-year-old woman returns two weeks after completing two applications of permethrin 5% cream for scabies. She reports persistent itching and examination reveals new burrows and mites on scrapings. She confirms all household contacts were treated simultaneously. What is the most appropriate next treatment?
options: Prescribe topical crotamiton 10% cream for two weeks
Repeat permethrin 5% cream twice, one week apart
Give oral ivermectin 200 mcg/kg as a single dose
Switch to malathion 0.5% aqueous lotion, two applications one week apart
questions.explanation: Treatment failure with permethrin (confirmed by new burrows/mites) warrants an alternative topical agent such as malathion. Repeating permethrin is less effective after failure, and ivermectin is reserved for crusted scabies or outbreaks.

questions.stem: A 22-year-old medical student presents to GP with a 3-week history of intense itching, worse at night. Examination reveals several 5–10 mm burrows in the web spaces of her fingers and on her wrists. She has no significant past medical history. What is the single most appropriate next step?
options: Refer to dermatology for confirmation of diagnosis before treatment
Arrange skin scraping for microscopy to confirm scabies mites
Prescribe permethrin 5% cream for application from neck to toes, to be repeated after 7 days
Prescribe oral antihistamines for symptom control and review in 2 weeks
Advise washing all bedding and clothing at 60°C and treat only if symptoms worsen
questions.explanation: The clinical diagnosis of scabies is clear with typical burrows and nocturnal itch. NICE recommends treating empirically with permethrin 5% cream as first-line; a second application after 7 days is standard. Delaying treatment for investigations or referral is inappropriate when a highly effective, safe treatment exists.

questions.stem: A 78-year-old female resident of a nursing home presents with a widespread itchy papular rash of 4 weeks' duration. Several other residents and staff have similar symptoms. On examination, burrows are noted on her wrists and axillae. She has no signs of crusted scabies. What is the single most appropriate next step?
options: Prescribe oral ivermectin for all symptomatic individuals
Advise all residents and staff (including asymptomatic) to apply permethrin 5% cream, and repeat after 7 days
Isolate the index case and treat only those in direct contact
Advise all symptomatic residents and staff to apply permethrin 5% cream once
Treat the index case only with permethrin 5% cream
questions.explanation: In institutional outbreaks, NICE recommends treating all residents and staff (even asymptomatic) to prevent ongoing transmission. A single treatment is insufficient; a second application after 7 days is needed. Ivermectin is second-line and typically reserved for crusted scabies or when topical treatment fails.

questions.stem: A 65-year-old man with untreated HIV (CD4 50 cells/µL) presents with widespread thick crusts on his hands, feet, and scalp, with nail involvement. He is febrile (38.5°C) and has cervical lymphadenopathy. There is a strong smell from the skin. What is the single most appropriate next step?
options: Apply permethrin 5% cream daily for 7 days
Admit to hospital for isolation and combined topical and oral therapy
Prescribe a course of oral antibiotics for presumed secondary infection
Refer to dermatology outpatient clinic within 2 weeks
Start oral ivermectin as an outpatient
questions.explanation: Crusted scabies in an immunocompromised patient is a medical emergency due to high mite burden, risk of secondary infection and sepsis, and need for strict isolation. NICE recommends admission for combined therapy (topical permethrin + oral ivermectin) and supportive care. Outpatient management is unsafe.

questions.stem: A 30-year-old roofer presents with a 2-week history of itchy rash consistent with scabies. On examination, there are multiple excoriated papules and honey-coloured crusts over his forearms and lower legs, suggesting impetigo. He is systemically well. What is the single most appropriate next step?
options: Arrange wound swab and await culture before starting antibiotics
Prescribe oral flucloxacillin and topical permethrin to be started on the same day
Prescribe oral antibiotics alone and treat scabies only if itch persists
Prescribe permethrin 5% cream and review in 1 week
Refer to dermatology for combined management
questions.explanation: When scabies is complicated by bacterial superinfection (impetigo), both conditions require immediate treatment. NICE advises treating the infection (oral flucloxacillin) concurrently with the scabicide (permethrin). Delaying either treatment risks worsening infection or ongoing transmission.

questions.stem: A 28-year-old woman at 20 weeks gestation (primigravida) presents with a 2-week history of intense nocturnal itching and several burrows on her wrists and between her fingers. She is otherwise well. What is the single most appropriate next step?
options: Advise that no treatment is safe in pregnancy and refer to obstetric medicine
Defer treatment until after delivery to avoid fetal risk
Prescribe permethrin 5% cream as first-line treatment
Prescribe oral ivermectin as first-line treatment because it avoids topical application
Recommend benzyl benzoate lotion as an alternative to permethrin
questions.explanation: Permethrin 5% cream is safe in pregnancy and is first-line for scabies. Ivermectin is contraindicated in the first trimester (unlicensed), and benzyl benzoate is less well studied. NICE recommends treating promptly to reduce itching and prevent transmission; deferring treatment is not appropriate.

questions.stem: A 24-year-old medical student presents with intense pruritus, worse at night, and a rash on the wrists and between fingers. On examination, you see burrows and papules. Which single finding would raise concern for a life-threatening complication?
questions.explanation: Widespread crusted plaques indicate crusted scabies, a severe variant with heavy mite burden, leading to risk of secondary bacterial infection and sepsis. Other options are common in scabies but not specific for life-threatening complications.

questions.stem: A 30-year-old woman treated with permethrin 5% cream two weeks ago for scabies returns with persistent itch. Which finding suggests treatment failure rather than post-scabetic itch?
questions.explanation: New burrows indicate active mite infestation, confirming treatment failure. Persistent papules, excoriations, eczematous changes, and post-scabetic nodules are common after successful treatment due to ongoing hypersensitivity.

questions.stem: A 75-year-old nursing home resident with dementia and poor hygiene presents with widespread thick crusted plaques on hands, feet, and scalp. He is febrile (39°C) and hypotensive (80/50 mmHg). Which complication is most likely?
questions.explanation: Crusted scabies predisposes to bacterial superinfection, typically with Streptococcus or Staphylococcus, leading to bacteremia and septic shock. Fever and hypotension in this setting make septic shock the most likely complication.

questions.stem: A 45-year-old man with scabies on his trunk and arms develops sudden onset of severe pain and swelling in his left lower leg. On examination, there is erythema and warmth, but the pain seems out of proportion to the physical findings. What is the most likely diagnosis that requires immediate surgical intervention?
questions.explanation: Pain out of proportion to visible inflammation is a classic red flag for necrotizing fasciitis, a surgical emergency. Cellulitis typically has proportionate pain. DVT, pyoderma gangrenosum, and gout are less acute and lack this feature.

questions.stem: A 6-month-old infant with scabies presents with widespread erythema, superficial blisters, and skin peeling, especially around the mouth and diaper area. What is the most likely complication?
questions.explanation: Staphylococcal scalded skin syndrome (SSSS) is a life-threatening complication of staphylococcal infection, often secondary to scabies-related impetigo. The pattern of widespread erythema, blisters, and peeling, particularly periorally and in the diaper area, is characteristic. Atopic dermatitis lacks this acute desquamation; toxic shock syndrome has fever and hypotension; Kawasaki disease has conjunctivitis and oral changes; eczema herpeticum has vesicular lesions.
